The purpose of this training is to give you that ability, by giving you a deeper insight into how the pieces fit together and especially the last two, action threshold and pain threshold. Let me give you a quick of the definitions here. Channogies and I'll reinforce. Action threshold represents the collective level of certainty that a prospect needs to be at before they feel comfortable enough. They feel good enough. They feel justified to take action. In other words, someone like myself that has a very low action threshold, right? I don't have to be at a 10, 10, 10. I don't have to be absolutely, positively certain about. I can be like, yeah, it seems pretty good, right? And I'm like, I'll buy it. Now I'm not going to buy something that I think will hurt me because there's always this overarching principle that governs us of positive intent, right? But that being said, if I'm just marginally certain that my life will be improved or something not destroyed by this thing and also with the good feeling and shot of endorphins I get when I just buy shit, how I am as most people are, right? We love to buy. We hate to be sold to, right? So that's my low action threshold. It's constructed. It's comprised of the sum of all the beliefs I have about making decisions, about trusting people, both salespeople and just people in general, are my best decisions made quickly or should they be made slowly? That's really what my action threshold is made of. Then on the other side of the equation, I always use my dad. I love to death. He's getting very old, very sad. I love him so much. And the pain threshold means that essentially human beings, as we're built, when we're feeling severe pain or discomfort or angst, we will tend to act. And if it really is very severe, we will act. He was a great name. Yeah, because massive pain. That's what massive pain does to someone. And what's technically happening here, what you need to understand is that the impact the pain has is it causes the fulcrum to slide over to this side of the equation. And once it makes that slide, guess what? All of a sudden, by sheer physics and gravity, all of those major negatives, one second, I'll take a sip. All of those major negatives, they lose their power, they lose their force. The force they exert on the scale is diminished by a factor of like, you know, 10 or 1000. How far it's like, magnitude, it's reduced by and suddenly each little positive has a massive influence on the situation. Nothing lowers a person's action threshold more than pain. Now, that being said, remember, you know, when you're gathering intelligence from someone, right, you need to be essentially digging out pain during that process. You got you have to do that because there's two reasons why there's really, there's two places where we really want to really amplify pain and identify it. First, identify the name. Right. Initially, it's during your intelligence gathering. After you've asked your noninvasive questions, and you start getting down some of your more invasive ones, they could be big picture, like, you know, what's, you know, it could start like, you know, what's, you know, what's the one thing about this current service that you have that you're not really liking, right? That's not doing it for you. And that might elicit. Well, it's causing misproth, and you might hit a pain point. And they say, well, really tell me more about that. Oh, my God. Oh, wow. So tell me, and then you, and then what you want to do there is you want us to digging in with specific, more invasive questions that essentially amplify or at least bring out that pain point into the open. You bring it to the surface. Now, that's very important for a couple of reasons. Number one, that when someone is feeling pain, and you're presenting your solution, they're listening to you, they're hanging on you everywhere because they're listening from the perspective of someone who really is feeling uncomfortable and just wants to feel better. Think about that. Like when you go to the doctor and you're, if you're really sick, just doctors, please, what do I got to do versus your annual checkup? We're like, all right, well, you know, yeah, go take these fucking Omega three fucking pills. Oh, thanks, Doc. Maybe maybe not versus you're about to fucking have your appendix first, Doc, please cut me open and take out my appendix. I'm in pain. I can't function. So by our nature that by when we have a pain that's really out in the open and someone presents a solution to us, we're like, okay, I'll do it. So when you gather intelligence, if you go too quickly and don't take the time to really ferret out where their pain or uncomfortability lies, they're, they're feeling of being out of control, they're a lack. Where is that law? What does it really mean to them? And then kind of dig into amplify by asking more questions. What will happen is if you, or even worse, by the way, sometimes you see a rookie sales person that they've, they've, they've read, they've done some straight line training and they've heard about this stuff. They'll dig out a pain point. They'll be, oh, oh, guess what? My project is perfect. Don't even, Bill, calm down now. Rest assured, I got you covered. What happens when you do that? He's like, all right, well, really, okay, he's just feeling good again. And then when you all for your solution, remember the syntax, right? You, you gather intelligence and bill report at the same time and you do your transition, then you all for your solution comes after. So now, because you jumped the gun here and try to tell them, oh, how wonderful, don't worry, Bill, please, don't feel any angst, please stop worrying. You got me. He's, he now listens to your solution with a far less, let's say, um, um, um, a far more measured eye. He's no longer like, what is it? I want to get it. You've taken him out of pain. That's why you do not resolve pain in your qualifying session. You just don't do that. You want to amplify pain. Say, uh-huh. You take mental notes. The place you go about resolving that pain is when you actually make your presentation. This way, when you say, well, Jim, John, Jill, Jose, Mick, Wang, Wing, Wang, you're based on everything you just said to me. This is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. And now they're like, let me hear and they're hanging on your every word and they're judging everything you say, every feature, every benefit, the value proposition, the cost of benefit ratio, everything, how it measures against the competition, the judging all of that, every last bit of it to feeling pain versus they're listening to you like feeling good, feeling strong, feeling as what? A buyer in, that's why you're called a buyer in power. They feel power. They're in power versus a buyer in heat. They feel urgency. They got to take action now. They got to resolve the pain point. That's the difference. So you don't want to resolve pain as you qualify. You want to amplify them and say, well, Jim, let me say this based on everything you just said to me. Wow. This is the perfect, this is right up your eye. Let me tell you exactly why. And then you make your presentation. So that's the first spot is when you, when you basically, you know, our gathering, I told you how to dig out that pain point. Okay. Now, when the second spot, well, the next, now, let me just stop. Now remember, when I told that spots on the line, I'm talking about places where we're predominantly doing one thing. You're always doing all things at once, meaning you're always trying to stay in rapport. You're always taking control of the sale. You're always asking more questions through this. And this, I just don't never be too rigid in the application of the straight line, or else it falls apart. But the point is, there are all these spots where we're predominantly doing a certain thing. So when we gather and tell you what one of the things that we're doing in a major way is we're looking to dig out pain and amplify that pain. Remember, when I say amplify it, people are often in denial. In other words, the way pain works, right, is what it hurts, right, and makes us feel like shit. It's designed to do that pain, just as pain, bodily pain, discomfort, even emotional discomfort is an evolutionary feature biomechanically designed to help us actually live and not die. In other words, we have these warning systems. Hey, you know what? Something hurts here. I have to slow down. I have to protect this thing. I got to stop. I got to look a certain way. I'm hungry right now because it makes, and it's this, it's not, I'm uncomfortably hungry, which means what? Stop doing what you're doing and go eat some food. I'm dying of thirst means what? I got to drink some water because of it or else I'll die. These are all designed to allow you to live and function healthy in the world. Same thing is true of the pain that you feel emotionally, except when it gets debilitating like it's depression, and it's something to get pain, and I've been, by the way, and I've been to this myself, right, was in chronic pain with someone in my back where you get into this pain loop and it's pain for pain sake. That's a different thing. So sometimes someone gets in chronic pain and there's a loop forms that it's no longer serving you. So the pain is not warning you of anything. You're already healed. This is very common in your body, by the way. It's terrible thing. They've learned how to treat it more effectively. I had this in 1993, fucking destroyed out of fusion. It's a disaster, right? But I had pain for pain sake. Nothing structurally wrong with my body, but my brain is saying you're in pain. Great excuse for taking quaillus, the only upside, right? Anyway, going forward with the story, the point is, is that for the most part in the absence of a pain loop that doesn't serve anything, pain is designed to make you aware that you must do something to be well again, to feel good again. That's what it's there for. So when you amplify someone's pain, what's happening is saying you got to act pal, you got to act, because you know, you're not right right now, you got to get back to feeling right again. Now, what happens in that this is very important, why we suppose to amplify pain is that after a certain amount of time, if the pain is prolonged, and you're not doing anything about it, you try to fix it, it doesn't work. Guess what happens? We become numb to our own pain, we become comfortably numb, as Pink Floyd would say, where we essentially ratchet down our expectations of what is good. We'll say, you know what, I'm not really in pain, this is just, this is my life, it sucks. I shouldn't be happy and joyous. You know, that's, and you'll have all these reasons, your brain goes, it hurts, pain hurts, makes you, you can't go on. So either you act on pain to resolve it, or eventually guess what happens, you go into denial. The beauty of the straight line system, why it's ethical to do this is that someone's in denial, man, they live in a life of lack for no other reason that they're just in this terrible loop where they just like, they've gone to denial and they've convinced themselves, they said, okay, when they're really not. So by making them step back into that pain and uncomfortability, what it does, brings it back up and puts them back in reality. So that's the first place we want to identify pain in that intelligence gathering section and amplify it, but not resolve it. Then now moving into the back half of the sale, right? So now let's talk about the action threshold. So we ran off first two loops. And if you know the straight line syntax, let's go through it in the back half, right? The first step in the back half is we deflect the initial objection. That's what we do, right? Then we run a loop to build more certainty. That loop always ends with you asking for the order now, which we do the second time. When you ask for the order of the second time, what can happen? Well, they could be, they can either say yes, they could say not interested, very rare, right? Or hit you with another objection or, you know, they hop, right? At which point you run another loop. That is your next step. You run a second loop, right? You'll deflect. Now you don't deflect the next objection. You have to answer that second objection head on. You run your loop, you go back, you build more certainty, except now on this loop, as it closes out, you're not going to ask for the order again before you lower the action threshold. So you take a crack now, a very big crack, lowering the actual threshold by using this pattern of what's the worst that could possibly happen. You can essentially hijack their strategy of running long, toxic movies and make them run a more realistic and more healthy strategy that will allow them to get something that they need. That's your next loop, right? But if they don't, sometimes, by the way, you do that and it works like a magic. It really is magical, right? But it doesn't always work. Sometimes you have those really tough buys. I mean, like my father, where it's just like, wow, you're like, holy Christ, like, you know, that you made massive progress. You can tell, you know, like, I can't believe this person is still not buying. What is wrong with that? Right? Well, get it, right? Those are the people with this, like, you know, literally the Supo, so watch, here's what might have happened. So here, let's visually look at this. So let's say you have like someone like my dad's ultra high action threshold person, right? So he starts off at his, his focus is almost here. It's like, it's like, almost no credence given to positives and even one nasty negative is enough to plunk the scale down against you, right? Now you run this action threshold lowering pattern in your previous loop. Now just remember, let's just be clear what's what, when you're moving someone down the straight line, all the things you're saying and doing, you're not changing his action threshold yet. That's knocking off negatives and building on positives, right? Your previous patterns are about knocking out negatives and building positives, hoping to tip the scale like that. Now in the second, the second loop, now you're addressing the focus, the position of the focus and let's say that my dad, you ran that pattern and it moved the focus to here, moved it to be one or two notches down. But let's say that's still the cause of of any, maybe he had a real couple of nasty negatives still, or maybe really was this emotionally serious, doesn't matter. You, it's still not enough. It's those situations, people like that, that's when adding on pain, using the pain threshold, that's when the magic happens right there. So in other words, you've now been through your second loop and you've lowered the action threshold using words and future pacing, still not quite there yet. That's when you want to run a, and you'll get hit with an objection of some sort. All right. And you're going to loop back one more time, build certainty again, because there's going to be certainty ways. You have to reestablish certainty again, because certainty will not last very long. It lasts for maybe few minutes, it's just a deteriorate. That's just how human beings are built, right? So you button up certainty in this loop here and your third loop now, you're going to future pace someone, okay, lowest action threshold. And then you'll say, let me ask you a question. You know, when we spoke before, you mentioned, you know, you mentioned earlier that and you go back to the beginning of the sale when you were gathering intelligence, and you re insinuate that pain point, make them step back into that pain point and start making them talk about it by asking you open ended question. And let me just tell you one thing here, bottom line, people love to talk about their own fucking pain. It's a human thing. It's not it's not that one person's better, good. It's just human beings. It's our bill. We love to talk about our tale of whoa, expound on how bad things are. We love to pick at our own scabs, so to speak. That's how human beings are built. So it's really not a very difficult thing to get someone talking about what bothers them, what ails them, what aches them. Just got to say, tell me about it with that. Now remember, if you are not in rapport, they will not do it. If you're in very tight rapport, and you ask that question, the rest of them, you know, you spoke with, tell me what that you know, what do you see yourself in two years? How much does it really impact your life? And now, oh, people, they just trust they will start, they will go on and on digging out and as they're doing that, you're like, uh-huh. And you're using your active listening to say, I care, understand you, I feel your pain. That's what's happening. And as you're doing that, as they start talking, guess what's happening? They start feeling more and more pain. They start, they start stepping into their own pain. And as they do that, the action threshold starts to drift naturally to the right. So after you've done this, and you've had them literally, you know, really step into their pain like that, right? Then what you want to do is say, I totally get that. And you know you got that first number in the buying combination or the safes combination, right? But you don't pull down the handle, you don't try to open the safe because you're aware that the safest got more than one number, right? It's just why why would you do it? You wouldn't do it, it makes sense, right? Same thing goes when you then spin the number in the, the tumbling the opposite direction. Click, you get a second number, right? You got the first two numbers locked down tight. They must love your product. They must trust you. That's your numbers with, with human influence, right? But would you ask for the order? No, why? The fucking master locks, not three numbers, right? The most simple ones have three because that's the outside, it just be too easy to do, do a, you can guess them by just trying all the combinations. Three becomes difficult, right? So you spin it in the opposite direction. You hear that third click, what do you do now? You pull down on the handle and try to open the safe. Sometimes it opens. It opens when you have somebody with a very low action threshold, like me. Why? Because yeah, I still have two numbers left. You don't need to work with me. You got enough. You've done enough. I'm positive enough. I'm certain enough in the first three I can buy. You'll never have to address my fourth and fifth number. Sometimes you might, but not in this situation. But also you put more likely, if you were listening carefully, it means, ah, I've got a bit more of a complex safe here. I got a fourth number, probably a fifth one too. So you're going to start from the beginning again. You're going to loop back, make sure you're going to, you're going to run your pants to shore up the first 10, shore up the second 10, shore up the third 10. You got to make sure those three numbers have certainty wins. So you spin back on them, get those first three. Now you spin again for the fourth one. Click. That's when you lower the action threshold, when you expand that pattern. You click, you hit it. Now you try again. And by the way, very often you'll find most safes have four numbers in their combination, the safe of the human mind for buying. Some don't. Some are like my dad. You got to go to that fifth number. So you'll start again. You'll loop back, you'll shore up your first four numbers, make sure you got them locked down tight.
